You're right to push back.

If we're following the Real Python style and the assignment requirements, the goal is not to inspect random snippets forever. The goal is:

Load page
↓
Parse page
↓
Find all relevant elements
↓
Extract everything
↓
Save to JSON
↓
Clean later

That's exactly what the assignment says:

Parse the rendered HTML using BeautifulSoup, regex, and/or Python string methods.

Clean and structure the extracted records into JSON.

So let's stop adding debug prints.

The next thing we need is to identify the repeating applicant element. Since you've already generated:

Python
html = driver.page_source
soup = BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser")

the next action should be in the browser inspector or the saved HTML:

  1. Open GradCafe survey page.

  2. Right-click one applicant result.

  3. Click Inspect.

  4. Find the outermost repeated container for one result.

Paste me either:

  • a screenshot of the inspector, or

  • 20–30 lines of HTML surrounding one applicant entry.

Once we have that, we'll immediately write:

Python
results = soup.find_all(...)

and start filling:

Python
records = []

with real applicant data instead of placeholders.

That's the shortest path to applicant_data.json.

You said:

why the 500 and 1000 i do not see this in the example

ChatGPT said:

You're right.

The 500 and 1000 were debugging values I introduced. They are not from the example.

The example does something much simpler:

Python
title_index = html.find("<title>")
start_index = title_index + len("<title>")
end_index = html.find("</title>")
title = html[start_index:end_index]

So if we follow the example exactly, we'd do:

Python
result_index = html.find("/result/")

print(result_index)

Then see if it finds anything.

Or:

Python
accepted_index = html.find("Accepted")

print(accepted_index)

The pattern is:

1. Find something
2. Save the index
3. Extract around that index

The problem is we don't yet know what string uniquely identifies one applicant record.

So before writing extraction code, I'd ask:

When you look at the GradCafe page in the browser, what do you see repeated for every entry?

For example:

University Name
Program Name
Accepted
Fall 2026

or something else?

Once we know the repeated text pattern, we can use the exact same .find() approach shown in your lecture screenshot instead of me inventing offsets.

You said:

this is the approach i hav echosen Your solution SHOULD: - Use BeautifulSoup, Python string methods, and/or regex to find and extract necessary data components. - Use Selenium to load Grad Cafe pages when applicant results are dynamically rendered, paginated, or difficult to access through static urllib requests alone. - Use Selenium only as a browser-rendering tool for publicly accessible pages. - Use a hybrid scraping workflow when appropriate: 1. Use urllib to construct, inspect, and manage Grad Cafe URLs. 2. Use Selenium to open the page if browser rendering is needed. 3. Wait for applicant results or relevant page elements to load. 4. Extract the rendered HTML using Selenium’s page_source. 5. Parse the rendered HTML using BeautifulSoup, regex, and/or Python string methods. 6. Clean and structure the extracted records into JSON. - Use explicit waits when using Selenium, such as waiting for a relevant page element to appear. - Avoid relying only on hard-coded sleep calls when Selenium explicit waits are available. - Include reasonable delays or throttling between page requests. - Include selenium in requirements.txt if Selenium is used. - Document in the README whether the scraper used urllib-only requests, Selenium-rendered pages, or a hybrid urllib + Selenium + BeautifulSoup workflow. - Document in the README which browser/driver setup was used if Selenium was used, such as Chrome + ChromeDriver, Firefox + GeckoDriver, or Selenium Manager. - Be written using either functions or class methods. Your solution SHOULD include the following functions or methods: - scrape_data(): pulls data from Grad Cafe. - clean_data(): converts scraped data into a structured format. - save_data(): saves cleaned data into a JSON file. - load_data(): loads cleaned data from a JSON file. Your solution SHOULD also: - Use private helper methods where appropriate, indicated with an underscore before the method name, such as _parse_entry() or _normalize_status(). - Carry out scraping logic under scrape.py. - Carry out data cleaning under clean.py. - Ensure the final data does not include remnant HTML tags or HTML entities. - Handle unexpected, inconsistent, or messy information gracefully. - Preserve information accurately and avoid changing applicant-provided data in ways that are not supported by the website. - Maintain raw fields alongside cleaned fields where helpful for reproducibility. - Be well commented and clear. - Use appropriately named variables.

convert this to the new logic from urllib import parse, robotparser import json from selenium import webdriver from bs4 import BeautifulSoup # Confirm robots.txt permits scraping agent = "rida" base_url = "https://www.thegradcafe.com/" parser = robotparser.RobotFileParser() parser.set_url(parse.urljoin(base_url, "robots.txt")) parser.read() print(parser.can_fetch(agent, base_url)) # Use urllib to construct and manage GradCafe URL survey_url = parse.urljoin(base_url, "survey") print(survey_url) # Use Selenium to open the page driver = webdriver.Chrome() driver.get(survey_url) print(driver.title) # Extract rendered HTML using Selenium page_source html = driver.page_source print(type(html)) # Parse rendered HTML with BeautifulSoup soup = BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser") print(type(soup)) print(soup.title.text) # Create JSON output structure records = [] with open("module_2/applicant_data.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as file: json.dump(records, file, indent=4) print("Saved applicant_data.json") driver.quit()

ChatGPT said:

Replace scrape.py with this:

Python
from urllib import parse, robotparser
import json

from selenium import webdriver
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup


BASE_URL = "https://www.thegradcafe.com/"
OUTPUT_FILE = "module_2/applicant_data.json"


def check_robots():
    agent = "rida"

    parser = robotparser.RobotFileParser()
    parser.set_url(parse.urljoin(BASE_URL, "robots.txt"))
    parser.read()

    return parser.can_fetch(agent, BASE_URL)


def scrape_data():
    survey_url = parse.urljoin(BASE_URL, "survey")
    print(survey_url)

    driver = webdriver.Chrome()
    driver.get(survey_url)

    print(driver.title)

    html = driver.page_source
    print(type(html))

    soup = BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser")
    print(type(soup))
    print(soup.title.text)

    records = []

    driver.quit()

    return records


def save_data(data, filename):
    with open(filename, "w", encoding="utf-8") as file:
        json.dump(data, file, indent=4)

    print(f"Saved {filename}")


def load_data(filename):
    with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8") as file:
        return json.load(file)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(check_robots())

    applicant_records = scrape_data()

    save_data(applicant_records, OUTPUT_FILE)

This keeps your current working logic, but now matches the required function structure:

  • scrape_data()

  • save_data()

  • load_data()

Next we’ll replace records = [] with real extracted rows.

module_2/

Python

is only temporary while we're building.

Eventually:

Python
applicant_records = scrape_data()
save_data(applicant_records, OUTPUT_FILE)

will create:

module_2/applicant_data.json

containing the 30,000+ applicant records required by the assignment.

So the filename is already correct; what we're missing is populating applicant_records with actual scraped data.

Python

and then:

Grad School Admissions Results for 2006–2026 • thegradcafe.com - The GradCafe

which means:

Python
driver = webdriver.Chrome()

is already functioning on your machine.

Since one of the requirements is:

Document which browser/driver setup was used if Selenium was used.

either approach is acceptable.

For class consistency, I'd only switch to the Service + ChromeDriverManager() pattern if:

  1. It's shown in your course materials, and

  2. You add the package to requirements.txt.

Otherwise, keep the working version and note in the README that you used:

Selenium Manager + Chrome

which is what Selenium 4 does automatically. That keeps us focused on the real missing requirement: extracting applicant records into applicant_data.json.

ChatGPT said:

This actually supports the approach you've chosen.

The example shows Selenium being used as a browser automation tool:

Python
drv = webdriver.Chrome()
drv.get("https://www.google.com/")

For your assignment, the equivalent is:

Python
driver = webdriver.Chrome()
driver.get(survey_url)

Then instead of:

Python
box = drv.find_element(...)

your assignment wants:

Python
html = driver.page_source
soup = BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser")

because the goal is not to search Google—it's to extract GradCafe data.

So your scrape_data() should eventually look conceptually like:

Python
def scrape_data():

    survey_url = parse.urljoin(BASE_URL, "survey")

    driver = webdriver.Chrome()
    driver.get(survey_url)

    html = driver.page_source

    soup = BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser")

    # extract applicant records here

    driver.quit()

    return records

That matches:

  1. urllib URL construction

  2. Selenium opens page

  3. page_source

  4. BeautifulSoup parsing

  5. extract records

  6. JSON save later

which is exactly the workflow you selected from the requirements.

The next thing we need is not more Selenium code. We need to identify the HTML element containing:

School | Program | Added On | Decision

because that's what will drive the extraction into applicant_data.json.


You said:

lets implements this now

ChatGPT said:

Yes. Based on your chosen workflow and requirements, replace scrape.py with this:

Python
from urllib import parse, robotparser
import json

from selenium import webdriver
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup


BASE_URL = "https://www.thegradcafe.com/"
OUTPUT_FILE = "module_2/applicant_data.json"


def check_robots():
    agent = "rida"

    parser = robotparser.RobotFileParser()
    parser.set_url(parse.urljoin(BASE_URL, "robots.txt"))
    parser.read()

    return parser.can_fetch(agent, BASE_URL)


def scrape_data():
    # Use urllib to construct GradCafe URL
    survey_url = parse.urljoin(BASE_URL, "survey")

    # Use Selenium to open the page
    driver = webdriver.Chrome()
    driver.get(survey_url)

    # Extract rendered HTML
    html = driver.page_source

    # Parse rendered HTML
    soup = BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser")

    # Temporary: raw page text until we identify each row/card
    raw_text = soup.get_text(" ", strip=True)

    records = [
        {
            "program_name": None,
            "university": None,
            "comments": None,
            "date_added": None,
            "url": None,
            "applicant_status": None,
            "acceptance_date": None,
            "rejection_date": None,
            "start_term": None,
            "student_type": None,
            "gre_score": None,
            "gre_v": None,
            "degree": None,
            "gpa": None,
            "gre_aw": None,
            "raw_text": raw_text
        }
    ]

    driver.quit()

    return records


def save_data(data, filename):
    with open(filename, "w", encoding="utf-8") as file:
        json.dump(data, file, indent=4)

    print(f"Saved {filename}")


def load_data(filename):
    with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8") as file:
        return json.load(file)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(check_robots())

    applicant_records = scrape_data()

    save_data(applicant_records, OUTPUT_FILE)

Run:

Bash
export SSL_CERT_FILE=$(python -m certifi)
python module_2/scrape.py

This creates module_2/applicant_data.json, but it is still only a raw first pass. Next we replace the single raw-text record with real extracted rows/cards.
